summary: This devlog details a series of performance optimizations that dramatically improved Ghostty's IO throughput. The improvements include SIMD-based parsing that processes plain text 7-16x faster, precomputed lookup tables for Unicode codepoint width calculation that eliminated 30% of character processing time, and optimized grapheme break detection that runs nearly 8x faster while using less than 1KB of memory. A community contributor also implemented CSI sequence fast-path parsing for control-sequence-heavy applications. The combined optimizations result in Ghostty being faster at reading plain text with full grapheme clustering than most terminals that don't perform grapheme detection at all.
key_points: SIMD instructions allow processing 16-32 bytes simultaneously instead of one byte at a time, achieving near-memcpy speeds for parsing
Custom precomputed lookup tables for codepoint width are faster than even the simpler but incorrect libc wcwidth API
Terminal-specific preconditions (no control characters, ASCII filtering) enable grapheme break detection via a sub-1KB lookup table
Real-world improvements ranged from 2-5x faster plain text throughput depending on UTF-8 complexity
Community contributions, particularly CSI fast-path parsing, improved control-sequence-heavy workloads by 1.4-2x
money_quotes: SIMD is a rare scenario where hand-writing assembly (or near it) often results in significantly better performance over a state of the art optimizing compiler.
Our SIMD pipeline is reading and parsing text at roughly the same speed as simply reading the data into memory.
Through profiling efforts, we determined that 30% of the time taken for every printed character was spent calculating codepoint width.
We now get correct results faster than the simple broken libc API.

I realized if you add some additional preconditions a terminal can guarantee, you can eliminate enough conditionals such that computing a lookup table of every possible codepoint pair plus state takes up less than 1KB of memory.
In almost every case Ghostty is faster at reading plain text while doing grapheme break detection over terminals that read plain text while doing nothing.
key_insight: By exploiting domain-specific constraints unique to terminal emulators—like guaranteed absence of control characters in certain parsing stages—you can achieve correctness and speed simultaneously, often outperforming simpler but less correct implementations.
